The Thinnest Blue Line
nytimes_articles.abstract:
RICHARD PENNINGTON LOOKS DOUR AS HE THREADS HIS way among the ponytails and little black dresses at Planet Hollywood in the French Quarter. The new Police Superintendent is wearing a splashy tie, and his double-breasted suit makes his  broad shoulders appear even more imposing than usual. But he remains silent as his wife, Rene Webb, stops to mingle. Scanning the room through wire-rimmed glasses, Pennington seems aloof, distracted. It has been 18 months since he was  brought in from Washington to clean up the New Orleans Police Department, which  some have called the worst in America. Yet the chief continues to maintain a wary distance from strangers, still not sure whom to trust. 

     You can hardly blame him. Only days after his arrival, Pennington learned that in recent years the department has behaved less like a police force than a  loose confederation of gangsters terrorizing sections of the city. The Federal Bureau of Investigation informed the chief that it had wiretapped one of his officers, Len Davis, as he allegedly ordered the killing of Kim Groves, a 32-year-old mother of three who had filed a police brutality complaint against him.

nytimes_articles.headline: LONG ISLAND JOURNAL

Nondining AloftTo the Editor: Re "Airline Food Keeps Shrinking" (Practical Traveler, Jan. 14) and Virginia K. Henry's letter about American Airlines' Bistro service (Feb. 25). On an 8 A.M. American Airlines flight Feb. 8 from Hartford, Conn., to Austin, Tex., with a stopover in Chicago, there was neither food nor Bistro service.    Upon check-in at Hartford I was told there would be breakfast. Beverage service was available but no food, not even a pretzel, from Hartford to Chicago. The flight attendants suggested that I deplane in Chicago and purchase food for  the Austin leg. I decided to wait for a very late Mexican lunch in Austin.

SPENDING IT;Celebrity Foundations In Glare of the Spotlight
nytimes_articles.abstract:
MICHAEL JORDAN is best known for his almost effortless command of the basketball court. But in the clubby sports world, he is also known as an astute  businessman who controls vast sums of money with equal ease. 

     So it was something of a surprise when Mr. Jordan, the star of the Chicago Bulls, announced earlier this month that he had become disappointed with the children's foundation he established seven years ago and was closing it down, as of today.

NEIGHBORHOOD REPORT: DOWNTOWN BROOKLYN;Tickets, Tow Trucks, Cat and Mouse
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Afternoons on Atlantic Avenue have a gamelike air between Court Street and Boerum Place, with merchants peering out their storefront windows for tow trucks, customers dashing in and out of shops and delivery drivers looking both  ways before making a drop-off. 

     "Wait, wait, wait," said Carlos Jordan, the owner of Breakaway Travel and Tours, as he hurried through the maze of desks in his office to get outside. "I  got to make sure they don't tow my car."

nytimes_articles.headline: Persistence Pays In Getting a Loan
STYLE;Fashion Hotel
nytimes_articles.abstract:
The first sign is the metal barricade that the police erect across from the entrance to hold back the camera-wielding groupies who camp out day and night, rain or shine. Next comes the caravan of florist trucks filled with bouquets of  wildflowers and white lilies, which the bellmen dutifully place on all available surfaces in each of the pale pink suites. 

     Then one morning, not long after dawn, the dark sedans arrive delivering the  socialites with their Vuitton luggage, the editors with their Prada knapsacks and the models with their chisel-faced boyfriends. For seven days, four times a  year, as sure as spring-summer follows fall-winter, the Ritz in Paris becomes the Fashion Hotel.

NEW YORKERS & CO.
Temples Open Their Doors So No One Will Be Alone for the Seder
nytimes_articles.abstract:
TWENTY-ONE years ago, Frieda Falk and several women she knew became concerned that the students at Adelphi and Hofstra would have no Passover seders to attend. The holiday fell mid-week during a regular school schedule, and there would not be enough time for students to travel home for the Passover holiday. 

     So Mrs. Falk and her friends organized a seder for them in a temple nearby. It turned out so well that Mrs. Falk and her friends formed the Shared Seder Steering Committee to continue the community seder for the students each year.

nytimes_articles.headline: Some Personalize Their Seder To Underscore Timely Events
nytimes_articles.abstract:
AS Gloria Fischel greeted guests to her Passover seder last year in East Hills, she handed each one a map, a toy airplane, and a compass to go on a symbolic tour of the Holy Land. 

     She also gave each person a pith helmet to wear, so that each man and woman could feel as if he or she was in ancient Egypt. She herself wore a long caftan  dress, the kind a woman in ancient Egypt might have worn. Her children, too, wore costumes of the Middle East.

A VIEW FROM BERKELEY
nytimes_articles.abstract:
When the debate over affirmative action in higher education started to simmer, the stance I took as Chancellor of the University of California at Berkeley seemed to surprise many people. 

     To be sure, my view -- that we should consider race, ethnicity and gender along with many other factors in admissions -- has put me at odds with some constituencies, including the majority of the Regents of the University of California, who govern my campus. Last July, these officials voted to end affirmative action admission policies.

ROAD AND RAIL;Jilted by Toll-Weary Truckers, the Turnpike Goes A-Wooing
nytimes_articles.abstract:
At least twice a week, Tom Chandler drives his 75-foot-long truck through New Jersey on Interstate 287, though it is not his road of choice. "Too rough," said Mr. Chandler, whose tractor-trailer is loaded sometimes with telephone books, sometimes with Chef Boyardee spaghetti. "It's hard on the tires, hard on the tie rods, hard on the shocks." 

     The New Jersey Turnpike, with its smooth, well-maintained surface, would suit him better. But Mr. Chandler has been avoiding it since truck tolls doubled five years ago. "I used to run it steady," Mr. Chandler said, "but I can't afford to  now."

BOOKEND;Rehabilitating a Monster
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Our restless culture propels itself as much by revivals as by new discoveries. This past season we benefited from the welcome return among us of Jane Austen and Vermeer. A few decades ago we rediscovered the beautifully written "Narrative" by Frederick Douglass, which provides a searing account of slavery from the inside. We can never again ignore it. Several centuries ago, the rediscovery of Greek and Latin classics helped usher in an era of humanistic and scientific exploration. We call it the Renaissance. 

     The process of revival goes on even while we're not looking. During the 20th  century, we have been kept busy with two hot wars and one cold, and with the rise of movies and television. During that period, a few critics set out to rehabilitate a malodorous 18th-century writer who, 100 years ago, had been not forgotten but assigned appropriately to an obscure corner. The revival of the Marquis de Sade, I believe, may turn out to be the most significant and insistent revival of the 20th century -- unless some of us resolve to challenge  it. Sade confronts us with the model case of how we have blurred the distinction between literature and pornography.

Backtalk;Who's on Verse?
nytimes_articles.abstract:
On June 3, 1888, "Casey at the Bat" first appeared quietly on the pages of The San Francisco Examiner. Since that time, it has become one of America's best-known and best-loved poems. An even though mighty Casey's failure at the plate is initially tragic and surprising, it is, perhaps, a comforting metaphor  for all of us in the Mudville masses. 

     As a baseball ballad, with its keen sense of the sport's everbuilding drama,  Ernest Lawrence Thayer's "Casey at the Bat" is perfection. But in another sense, it has plenty of company, for it is just one of tens of thousands of baseball poems, many of which are housed in the archives of the Baseball Hall of Fame Library in Cooperstown. Some of these efforts are pure doggerel, but others are  well-crafted verse from some of our country's best poets -- Robert Francis, Marianne Moore, May Swenson and Carl Sandburg. It is one more reminder, as a new season begins tonight, that baseball and poetry -- like some veteran pitcher and catcher -- have long been intertwined.

VOICES: FROM THE DESK OF;Let's Leave Daughters In School
nytimes_articles.abstract:
THE fourth annual Take Our Daughters to Work Day will be observed in just a few weeks, on Thursday, April 25. Started in 1993 by the Ms. Foundation for Women, it is meant to be an opportunity for girls between the ages of 9 and 15 to visit their parents or other adults at their places of business in order to observe what happens in the working world. 

     I was working in the Wall Street area on the first Take Our Daughters to Work Day in 1993. I remember seeing a lot of little girls (most of them seemed closer to 9 than 15) riding the train with their moms and dads. Many were dressed in their party best, obviously excited about spending a day in the big city.

Sunday March 31, 1996;A QUESTION FOR: Lynda La Plante
LIVES;My Inner Shrimp
nytimes_articles.abstract:
For the rest of my days, I shall be a recovering short person. Even from my lofty perch of something over six feet (as if I don't know within a micron), I have the soul of a shrimp. I feel the pain of the diminutive, irrespective of whether they feel it themselves, because my visit to the planet of the teen-age  midgets was harrowing, humiliating and extended. I even perceive my last-minute escape to have been flukish, somehow unearned -- as if the Commissioner of Growth Spurts had been an old classmate of my father. 

     My most recent reminder of all this came the afternoon I went hunting for a new office. I had noticed a building under construction in my neighborhood -- a  brick warren of duplexes, with wide, westerly-facing windows, promising ideal light for a working studio. When I was ushered into the model unit, my pulse quickened: the soaring, 22-foot living room walls were gloriously aglow with the remains of the day. I bonded immediately.

nytimes_articles.abstract:
WHY, IT MUST BE Giordanomania. On Saturday evening, the Metropolitan Opera presents a new production of "Andrea Chenier," starring Luciano Pavarotti. Then, "Chenier" will open the Met's next season, which also includes the company's first staging of "Fedora" in 70 years. Shades of 1925-26, when the repertory included three operas by Umberto Giordano, the Italian composer: "Chenier," "Fedora" and "La Cena Delle Beffe." 

     The reality, alas, seems more mundane. Mr. Pavarotti, who recorded the role to no great effect in the early 80's, has apparently decided that the time for his Chenier has come, even as Mirella Freni, in the twilight of her career, takes on Fedora, a role often associated with "veteran" sopranos.

The Wild Boy of Nuremberg
nytimes_articles.abstract: LOST PRINCE The Unsolved Mystery of Kaspar Hauser. Translated and introduced by Jeffrey Moussaieff Masson. Illustrated. 254 pp. New York: The Free Press. $23.    THERE are literally thousands of books and articles in German about Kaspar Hauser, the 19th-century German wild boy, and about everything that has anything to do with his case. There are poems by Verlaine and Trakl, a novel by Jakob Wassermann, a play by Peter Handke and an excellent movie made in 1974 by Werner Herzog. In far-off America, Herman Melville compared Billy Budd, the innocent, tongue-tied and doomed-victim sailor, to Kaspar. There are even books about the  books and articles devoted to the case -- it remains a kind of cultural cause clbre. Now there is "Lost Prince," by Jeffrey Moussaieff Masson, who at least in part has made something of a career by calling attention to himself. Mr. Masson  has produced an extremely eccentric volume on Kaspar Hauser. But since Kaspar's  story is not widely known in English, a brief summary of its principal features  may be useful at the outset.

nytimes_articles.abstract: AGAINST GRAVITY By Lucy Ferriss. 303 pp. New York: Simon & Schuster. $22.    IN small towns, dissimilar people are compelled to know one another over time. Yearnings, antagonisms, subterfuges and alliances occur among residents who cannot later become strangers. For many, that is a fair price to pay for life in a place that is rooted and decipherable.

NEIGHBORHOOD REPORT: CARROL GARDENS;'Mayor' Who Refused to Run
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Salvatore (Buddy) Scotto looks so much like Mayor Giuliani that strangers often mistake him for the Mayor. But Mr. Scotto seldom encounters strangers when strolling, driving, or just standing in his neighborhood. Many locals refer to Mr. Scotto as the Mayor of Carroll Gardens. 

     "He's gotten that name because he is someone people in the community look to  and respect," said Assemblywoman Eileen Dugan. "He never says no to anyone. It never ceases to amaze me."

nytimes_articles.headline: FOOD;Getting Sauced
nytimes_articles.abstract:
ONCE UPON A TIME, CONDIMENTS WERE THE the essence of a place, a distillation  of flavors that told the story of that land's cooking. But as the world grew smaller, condiments grew worldlier. Suddenly, mustard and ketchup have global identities. 

     This is also becoming true of soy sauce and black bean paste, which now lend  a generalized, international ambiance to a dish rather than a particular Cantonese nuance. Peanut sauce, on the other hand, is an exotic exception. This  Thai mixture of nuts, curry, coconut, soy and lime beloved by all of Southeast Asia is a culinary ambassador. It can make a dish taste of trade winds and palm  trees, of a scorching midday sun and an afernoon deluge, of perfumed evenings delicately tinged with spice.

VIEWS;Survivors: The Lives and Troubled Times of a Newark Tenement
nytimes_articles.abstract:
In the beginning I had no conscious reason for my interest in No. 322. Intuitively I felt drawn to the place, and it seemed important to photograph it  and the people who lived here. 

     It has been an education: Making my way through the dungeonlike, scarred hallways, barely able to see because just one dim bulb remains in a multiple light fixture. Walking up the narrow stairs that smell of mildew and rot -- decay that grows from lack of maintenance and lack of sunlight, which has no chance to stream through boarded-up windows. Steadying myself on the rotted banister. Sitting down and listening to people tell me their stories.

SOAPBOX;Strangers on a Train
nytimes_articles.abstract:
I'VE been commuting between New Jersey and Manhattan for 12 years same time (7:54), same car (fourth from last) and, insofar as possible, same seat (second  row, window). We commuters are creatures of habit, and we often sit next to the same person for years. 

     We start out as strangers. But the day-in, day-out challenges establish a closeness, sometimes even an intimacy. I've been confidante to a succession of seatmates, each revelation a simultaneously surprising and disturbing experience.

NEW YORKERS & CO.;Signs of the Medieval Times
nytimes_articles.abstract:
TRADE signs, which identify businesses and services with three-dimensional figures, are often quirky and individualistic as well as attention grabbing. Descendants of the guild signs of medieval Europe, they still grace modern streets in the form of the barber's pole, the pharmacist's mortar and pestle and the pawnshop's three balls. 

     In 18th-century New York, businesses were often referred to by their signs. A watchmaker, for example, was known as Sign of the Dial, and a printer as Sign of the Bible. When a pewter smith, the Sign of the Platter, moved from Dock Street  to Wall Street in 1744, it put a notice of the move in The New York Post-Boy of  May 7 stating that the firm "has the same sign."

nytimes_articles.headline: A Proposed Water Sale Stirs Opposition
The Stealth Candidate
nytimes_articles.abstract:
IF YOU LISTEN TO THE DEMOCRATS AND REPUBLICANS, THEY would have you believe that the 1996 Presidential race has already settled into a simple contest between Dole and Clinton. They ignore Ross Perot and his quest for a third party. Few Democrats and Republicans understand what his undertaking means for November. If they did, they would be worried. 

     "There is absolutely no demand for a third-party candidate," says Haley Barbour, the chairman of the Republican National Committee. "If we keep our promises in Congress, Perot won't be a factor." Yet the latest polls show as many as two-thirds of voters want a third party. The enthusiasm is not just from independents, but also from large numbers of Republicans and Democrats.

WEDDINGS;Debra Feldman, Christopher Kiah
Sold Out
nytimes_articles.abstract:
We've got the information age all wrong. Someone who reads today's newspapers would conclude that the four most important aspects of the information age are cyberporn, Windows 95, Newt Gingrich and cyberporn. This is like saying that the most important signposts during the rise of industrial capitalism in America were mass-produced pornographic magazines, Warren Gamaliel Harding and the Veg-O-Matic. 

     To understand the age we have entered, we need more than a modem, the Bill of Rights and a subscription to Penthouse on line. We need to figure out how the world changes when information becomes one of the most important forms of wealth and power: When everything from the pattern of purchases revealed by credit card receipts to the pattern of your D.N.A. can become a byte of information, to be bought and sold in the marketplace.

Stirrings
nytimes_articles.abstract: VTAL DUST Life as a Cosmic Imperative. By Christian de Duve. 362 pp. New York: Basic Books. Paper, $14.    WHEN Charles Darwin wrote the "Origin of Species," he deliberately refused to speculate about the first beginnings of life, claiming he hardly knew enough to  make any lasting contribution, and he held back from discussing what he really thought about the mental world of human beings, the highest point of the evolutionary process. Caution was necessary. Excited talk about spontaneous generation of insects occurring after electricity was run through overripe cheeses, and inflammatory, tub-thumping sermons on the miracle of divine creation, made the first stirrings of life on Earth the most perplexing and fascinating problem of the era. Similarly, to discuss the human mind as if its action were nothing more than the mechanical product of the brain might invite a storm of protest. Darwin therefore kept his evolutionary proposals inside strict self-imposed limits -- and was aghast when the hurricane that gathered raged around the very issues he studiously ignored. It is extraordinary how much more  is now known about these mysterious beginnings and endings, the parts of the evolutionary panorama that Darwin left out. Over the last 30 years or so the whole emphasis of research has shifted toward opening up the world of the minute, the vibrant hidden frontiers of life. Christian de Duve's "Vital Dust,"  published in 1995 and now reissued in a welcome paperback format, provides an astonishingly full account of this new story about life, as lucid and engaging as the "Origin" itself. Here is the entire sweep of what is known about living matter.

Congress Plays Doctor
nytimes_articles.abstract:
When the House took the unprecedented step last week of passing a bill that would outlaw a specific and safe surgical procedure, the so-called partial birth abortion, it set a dangerous precedent by inappropriately intruding into the clinical practice of medicine. 

     Medical decisions should be based on scientific evidence gleaned from laboratory and clinical evaluation. Procedures should be judged on safety, effectiveness, availability and affordability. Such decisions should not fall within the purview of ideology and politics.
